---
title: "How Footprints in Mud Exposed the Colonial Origins of Archeology"
short_title: "Footprints Archeology"
slug: "footprints-archeology"
description: "Human footprints at White Sands proved people lived in the Americas 23,000 years ago — 10,000 years before archeologists said was possible, exactly when Indigenous people said it was."

publish_date: "2026-03-17"
updated_date: ""
author: "Harold Young"
tags:
  - archaeology
  - science
  - colonialism
  - indigenous-history
series: ""
series_part:

cover_image: "images/cover.jpg"
og_image: "images/cover.jpg"

canonical_url: "https://medium.com/@halapenyoharry/how-footprints-in-mud-exposed-the-colonial-origins-of-archeology-abec71d44b0f"
syndication:
  medium: "https://medium.com/@halapenyoharry/how-footprints-in-mud-exposed-the-colonial-origins-of-archeology-abec71d44b0f"
  substack: ""
  linkedin: ""
  hackernews: ""
  reddit: ""

status: published
---

For many decades, archeologists taught that humans first migrated to the Americas 13,000 years ago. This theory was called the Clovis First model. Archeologists used this 13,000-year date as the maximum limit for human history in the region.

Indigenous people stated their ancestors lived in the Americas for much longer. Western academics ignored these statements. Archeologists labeled Indigenous histories as untrue because they did not match the 13,000-year limit determined by Western methods.

Researchers then found human footprints in dry mud at White Sands National Park in New Mexico. They tested seeds found inside the footprints. The tests proved the footprints were made 21,000 to 23,000 years ago. This physical proof showed that humans lived in North America at least 10,000 years earlier than archeologists had previously stated.

## The Physical Impossibility of the Land Bridge

The 23,000-year date directly conflicts with the physical geography of North America during that time. Earth was in the Last Glacial Maximum. Two continental ice sheets, the Laurentide and the Cordilleran, merged and covered the northern half of North America. This created a continuous physical barrier of ice thousands of feet thick.

Because the inland walking route was covered by ice, the humans who left the footprints in New Mexico had to use a different route. The inland path did not melt and open for walking until about 13,000 years ago. The only available path from Asia to the Americas was along the edge of the Pacific Ocean.

## Proof of Advanced Maritime Navigation

The Pacific coast 23,000 years ago was not a continuous walking path. Glaciers extended directly into the ocean, creating steep ice cliffs. To travel past these ice cliffs, humans had to use watercraft to move out onto the open ocean.

Operating boats in the open ocean requires specific technologies and knowledge systems:

- **Vessel Construction:** Humans required watercraft capable of surviving turbulent, freezing ocean water over long distances.
- **Celestial Navigation:** When traveling on the ocean away from visible land features, humans must use the positions of stars and planets to determine their direction.
- **Oceanic Knowledge:** Humans had to understand and utilize ocean currents and wind patterns to move their watercraft.

The existence of humans in New Mexico 23,000 years ago is physical proof that they possessed the vessels and the astronomical data required to navigate the Pacific Ocean. This places advanced maritime operations tens of thousands of years before recorded European ocean navigation.

## Procedural Bias and the Reduction of History

By requiring a 13,000-year limit, early archeologists reduced the recognized duration of Indigenous occupation. Categorizing Indigenous people as recent migrants benefited the political goals of colonial governments. Those governments used the reduced timeline to validate their decisions to acquire Indigenous land.

This event shows a procedural bias in how facts are verified. Western researchers believed their methods of examining physical items were the only correct way to record history. Because they had not found physical items older than 13,000 years, they decided the older Indigenous histories were false. They valued their own lack of physical evidence more than the direct historical records of the Indigenous populations.

## The Conclusion

Because archeologists accepted the 13,000-year limit, they stopped excavating when they reached that specific soil layer. They physically did not look deeper. As a result, the field spent decades accumulating a massive volume of artifacts from the 13,000-year timeline. This large volume of recent evidence will statistically outweigh the smaller amount of older evidence for many decades.

This procedural failure exposes how the daily practice of archeology is unduly influenced by financial interests compared to other sciences. Funding was directed toward excavations that supported the accepted timeline rather than funding deeper digging that might challenge it. The mud footprints provided the physical proof required to change this timeline, but the history of this disagreement shows that archeology operated to validate a Western view of history. The discovery proves the academic institutions were incorrect about the timeline, the land bridge, and the technological capabilities of early Americans, while the Indigenous communities were correct.

Billie Eilish was correct.
